repo_notes.content: Developer documentation only. Mirror the repo structure and module boundaries (packages/, apps/, mewbo_ha_conversation/) with minimal redundancy. Every page must include a meaningful Mermaid diagram that matches the page purpose. Name exact classes, functions, and module paths. Clarify that the API exposes polling; the CLI runs the runtime in-process (no polling); the web console connects via the REST API. Focus on system behavior, abstractions, and integration contracts rather than usage tips. Avoid anthropomorphic language when describing LLM behavior (use 'the model generates' not 'the AI decides').

pages.title: Overview & Repo Map
pages.purpose: Single entry point: repo goals, package and app boundaries, and how the major subsystems relate. Frames the architecture as a shared core engine consumed by multiple client interfaces, with a uniform extension system layered on top.

pages.title: Core Runtime & Orchestration (mewbo_core)
pages.purpose: The shared execution substrate that every client interface uses: SessionRuntime as the public facade, Orchestrator as the session driver, and ToolUseLoop as the single async loop that drives all LLM tool calls. This section covers the engine itself — the parts no client can opt out of.

pages.title: Session Runtime, Store & Event Log
pages.purpose: Session lifecycle management: RunRegistry, SessionStore (JSONL transcripts, tags, attachments), and the immutable-event-log foundation that makes features like fork and replay possible.

pages.title: Conversation Fork, Edit & Regenerate
pages.purpose: Point-in-time session branching via resolve_session(fork_from, fork_at_ts), edit-and-regenerate with replacement text, and the console/CLI surfaces that expose it.

pages.title: Tool-Use Loop Execution Engine
pages.purpose: ToolUseLoop.run() as the single async execution engine. Covers message construction, LLM invocation, tool dispatch (concurrency partitioning, timeouts), step budget enforcement, user steering, and the capability-gated catalog filtering that hides tools and agents the client cannot render.

pages.title: Agent Hypervisor & Sub-Agent Lifecycle
pages.purpose: AgentHypervisor dual-layer design (code-level reflexes plus a root-LLM brain), the 6-state agent lifecycle, AgentHandle/AgentResult contracts, non-blocking vs blocking spawn, bidirectional messaging, and graceful cleanup.

pages.title: Context Builder, Summaries & Compaction
pages.purpose: ContextBuilder pipeline from raw transcript to ContextSnapshot, token budget accounting, and the two compaction modes (FULL/PARTIAL) triggered both proactively and reactively.

pages.title: LLM Abstraction & Model Routing
pages.purpose: ChatModel protocol, build_chat_model(), LiteLLM multi-provider routing, per-role model selection, reasoning-effort configuration, model-cascade fallback, and proxy model prefix handling.

pages.title: Plan Mode & Task Decomposition
pages.purpose: Mechanisms that let the model think before acting and decompose complex requests: planning.py, task_master.py, and the exit_plan_mode session tool. Covers when plan mode is entered, how plans are reviewed, and how task decomposition coexists with the single-loop execution model.

pages.title: Data Models, Event Types & Payload Schemas
pages.purpose: Core data shapes: ActionStep, EventRecord, ToolResult, and the discriminated payload union that drives frontend component routing via the kind field.

pages.title: Multi-Project Workspaces
pages.purpose: How Mewbo handles multiple project directories: project_store.py, project resolution and switching, project-scoped MCP and instructions, and the surfaces that expose project selection (CLI command, API param, console picker).

pages.title: Project Instructions Loading
pages.purpose: The hierarchical CLAUDE.md / AGENTS.md discovery mechanism: discover_all_instructions() walks priority levels (user → project → rules → local), discover_subtree_instructions() indexes nested guidance, and the noload marker lets specific files opt out. Covers how project guidance reaches the system prompt and how subtree skills/instructions are surfaced for on-demand reading.

pages.title: Extension System
pages.purpose: How the engine is extended without being modified. Groups the cooperative mechanisms — skills, plugins, hooks, permissions, capabilities, session tools, slash commands — that let users, plugins, and clients shape behavior at runtime through stable contracts.

pages.title: Skills System
pages.purpose: SkillRegistry discovery (user and plugin directories), no-override semantics, skill catalog injection into the system prompt, and tool scoping via filter_specs().

pages.title: Plugin System & Marketplace
pages.purpose: PluginManifest format, discovery (installed, builtin_plugins/, marketplace), install/uninstall lifecycle, and how load_all_plugin_components() wires skills, hooks, agents, MCP servers, and session tools into a running session.

pages.title: Agent Definition Registry
pages.purpose: AgentDef dataclass, YAML frontmatter parsing, CC tool name mapping, capability-gated visibility, agent body variable substitution, and how agent_type in spawn_agent triggers registry lookup.

pages.title: Widget Builder Builtin Plugin
pages.purpose: The widget-builder builtin plugin: SubmitWidgetTool (a SessionTool that lints, emits widget_ready, and terminates the agent loop), the st-widget-builder sub-agent and skill, and how the console renders inline stlite widgets from widget_ready events. Acts as a worked example of the full extension stack.

pages.title: SessionTool Protocol
pages.purpose: The SessionTool Protocol in session_tools.py: per-agent plugin tools with an async handle() and a should_terminate_run() signal, managed by SessionToolRegistry. Built-ins like exit_plan_mode satisfy the same protocol so plugin and core tools share a uniform contract.

pages.title: Permissions, Hooks & Tool Execution Gates
pages.purpose: PermissionPolicy rules (including episodic approval), the approval callback chain, and HookManager lifecycle hooks (on_session_start, on_session_end, on_compact, pre/post tool use). Covers error isolation guarantees for hooks.

pages.title: Capabilities System & Feature Gating
pages.purpose: Client capability negotiation via the X-Mewbo-Capabilities header and the pure-function primitives in capabilities.py that filter agent, skill, and session-tool catalogs to only what the client can render.

pages.title: Slash Commands & User Commands
pages.purpose: The user-command subsystem in commands.py: a registry of slash commands (e.g. /help, /usage, /new, /switch-project, /fork, /tag, /compact, /tokens) reused by every interface — CLI, API, console, and chat adapters — so user steering behaves consistently across surfaces.

pages.title: Tool Layer (mewbo_tools)
pages.purpose: What the model can invoke: built-in local tools, external MCP tools, and LSP code intelligence — all unified under AbstractTool with kind-discriminated results so new tool types render in clients without protocol changes.

pages.title: Tool Registry & Spec System
pages.purpose: ToolRegistry, ToolSpec (schema, capabilities, factory), filter_specs() shared allowlist/denylist filtering, and specs_to_langchain_tools() conversion.

pages.title: Local Tools (File Edit, Shell, Read)
pages.purpose: AiderEditBlockTool, FileEditTool, AiderShellTool, AiderReadFileTool, and AiderListDirTool: their input formats, safe path resolution, and structured result shapes.

pages.title: Configurable Edit Tool Mechanism
pages.purpose: Two edit tool implementations (search_replace_block and structured_patch), auto-selection logic based on model identity, and shared edit_common.py utilities.

pages.title: MCP Tool Discovery & Schema Validation
pages.purpose: MCP config (global and project-scoped), MCPConnectionPool (persistent connections with auto-reconnect), MCPToolRunner, and schema normalization/coercion.

pages.title: Tool Search & Deferred Schemas
pages.purpose: Solves the context-window cost of large MCP catalogs: rather than binding every tool schema upfront, ToolSearchRunner advertises tool names cheaply and lets the model fetch the schemas it actually needs via a tool_search call, after which the loop re-binds the matched tools dynamically.

pages.title: LSP & Code Intelligence
pages.purpose: LSPTool operations (diagnostics, definition, references, hover), LSPManager lazy server spawning, built-in server definitions, and the passive diagnostic injection hook.

pages.title: Client Interfaces
pages.purpose: The client side of the architecture: every interface (REST API, web console, CLI, Home Assistant) is a different way of reaching the same core engine. This section covers what each interface provides on top of the engine and what its users can expect.

pages.title: REST API (mewbo_api)
pages.purpose: Flask-RESTX API overview: authentication, CORS, route structure, and how handlers bind to SessionRuntime and supporting stores. The API is the bridge layer for every non-CLI client (web console, Home Assistant, chat platforms) and for browser-based features like the Web IDE.

pages.title: API Endpoints, Polling & Steering
pages.purpose: Full endpoint inventory: session CRUD, async execution and event polling, user steering, plugin management, Web IDE lifecycle, git diff, and session fork.

pages.title: Chat Platform Adapters
pages.purpose: Extensibility surface for plugging chat platforms (Nextcloud Talk, Email today; Slack, Telegram, and others next) into the same engine. Defines the ChannelAdapter Protocol, the shared inbound pipeline (dedup, mention gating, session resolve, slash commands, LLM dispatch), and the conventions adapters follow for authentication, identity mapping, and reply rendering.

pages.title: Web IDE Integration
pages.purpose: Per-session code-server containers: IdeManager (Docker SDK spawn, extend, stop), IdeStore, nginx ide-proxy, and the console IdeLoader flow.

pages.title: Notifications & Share Tokens
pages.purpose: The two API-fronted stores that handle out-of-band signaling and external visibility: NotificationStore (notifications.py) for in-app notifications and push targets, and ShareStore (share_store.py) for read-only share URLs that expose a session to non-authenticated viewers.

pages.title: Web Console (mewbo_console)
pages.purpose: React 18 + Vite + Tailwind CSS frontend: session management, conversation timeline, ReviewPane, fork/edit-and-regenerate, PluginsView, Web IDE launch, and inline stlite widget rendering.

pages.title: Console UI Components
pages.purpose: Component hierarchy: base tool cards (LogEventCard, TerminalCard, DiffCard), hypervisor cards (CheckAgentsCard, SpawnAgentCard), widget cards (WidgetCard, StliteWidgetPanel), shared primitives, and the kind-discriminated log routing pipeline.

pages.title: Terminal CLI (mewbo_cli)
pages.purpose: CLI flow: Rich-based rendering, AgentDisplayManager for live agent tree display, DialogFactory for interactive prompts, in-process SessionRuntime usage, and slash commands.

pages.title: Home Assistant Integration (mewbo_ha_conversation)
pages.purpose: HA Conversation integration: async HTTP client, MewboApiClient, response parsing, and config flow.

pages.title: Configuration & Runtime Settings
pages.purpose: AppConfig hierarchy and all sub-configs: LLMConfig, AgentConfig (edit_tool, web_ide, tool_search, lsp), PluginsConfig, HooksConfig, and the MEWBO_HOME config resolution chain.

pages.title: CI/CD, Docker & Docs Pipeline
pages.purpose: GitHub Actions workflows, multi-arch Docker image builds (base/api/console), docker-compose with post-init scripts, and the MkDocs docs site build and deploy pipeline.

pages.title: Testing Patterns & Quality Gates
pages.purpose: Integration-first test philosophy: fake tools, AsyncMock LLM boundaries, full-turn harness tests. Covers test file organization, pre-commit hooks, and the asyncio.run() pattern used throughout.

pages.title: Tracing, Logging & Diagnostics
pages.purpose: Loguru logging, Langfuse session-scoped tracing, and the 4-layer session investigation methodology (MongoDB transcript → Langfuse → config → Docker).

pages.title: Building a New Client
pages.purpose: Step-by-step guide for new interface authors: initializing the registry and SessionRuntime, resolving sessions, handling slash commands and skills, choosing sync vs async execution, and consuming kind-discriminated events.

pages.title: Glossary
pages.purpose: Canonical one-sentence definitions for domain terms that appear across multiple pages without in-context definition (e.g., ToolUseLoop, AgentResult, SessionTool, session_capabilities, widget_ready, filter_specs).

page_notes.title: Overview & Repo Map
page_notes.notes: Frame the architecture as a shared core engine reached by multiple client interfaces, with a single extension system layered on top. The reader should leave understanding which parts are shared substrate (used by everyone) and which parts are interface-specific.

page_notes.title: Core Runtime & Orchestration (mewbo_core)
page_notes.notes: Motivate the single-async-loop choice over the more common planner/executor/synthesizer split. Position this section as 'the engine every client uses' and clarify that nothing here is interface-specific.

page_notes.title: Session Runtime, Store & Event Log
page_notes.notes: Treat the event log as the source of truth and explain how immutability enables features like fork. Clarify the sync (CLI in-process) vs async (API polling) split and why both share the same store.

page_notes.title: Conversation Fork, Edit & Regenerate
page_notes.notes: Discuss how the immutable event log makes branching cheap, and why per-message model override matters for iterative refinement. Reader should leave understanding the UX as a natural emergent property of the data model.

page_notes.title: Tool-Use Loop Execution Engine
page_notes.notes: Discuss how one loop coordinates LLM calls, tool dispatch, sub-agents, user steering, compaction, and budget enforcement. The model drives; the loop is substrate. Be explicit about what runs between steps and why.

page_notes.title: Agent Hypervisor & Sub-Agent Lifecycle
page_notes.notes: Explain why hypervision is split between an LLM brain (judgement) and code-level reflexes (zero-token watchdogs). Cover lifecycle states, the cost model, and why root uses non-blocking spawn while sub-agents block.

page_notes.title: Context Builder, Summaries & Compaction
page_notes.notes: Frame around the token-budget pressure problem. Explain why two compaction modes exist, when each fires, and how compaction stays invisible to the model when it works correctly.

page_notes.title: LLM Abstraction & Model Routing
page_notes.notes: Discuss the provider-agnostic design and the rationale for LiteLLM as a gateway. Cover per-role model selection, reasoning effort, model-cascade fallback, and how proxies are accommodated without tight coupling.

page_notes.title: Plan Mode & Task Decomposition
page_notes.notes: Discuss when planning is worth its cost and when it gets in the way. Cover how plan mode coexists with the single-loop architecture (it is not a separate engine) and how exit_plan_mode hands control back to execution. The reader should understand the tradeoff between deliberation and direct action.

page_notes.title: Data Models, Event Types & Payload Schemas
page_notes.notes: Center the discussion on the kind-discriminator pattern and how it lets backend and frontend evolve independently. Payload typing is the wire contract for every interface.

page_notes.title: Multi-Project Workspaces
page_notes.notes: Discuss why projects are a first-class concept (instructions, MCP servers, and CWD all scope to a project) rather than a CLI convenience. Cover how project resolution flows through the engine and how each surface lets a user switch contexts.

page_notes.title: Project Instructions Loading
page_notes.notes: Discuss how user, project, rules, and local instruction layers compose to form the system prompt's project context. Cover the noload marker as an explicit escape hatch and subtree discovery as a way to surface guidance without bloating the prompt.

page_notes.title: Extension System
page_notes.notes: Frame this section as the answer to 'how do I change behavior without modifying core?'. Discuss the design principle that every extension surface (skills, plugins, hooks, capabilities, session tools, slash commands) follows the same shape: stable contract, runtime discovery, no-override safety. The reader should leave able to pick the right extension surface for a given customization.

page_notes.title: Skills System
page_notes.notes: Position skills as runtime-extensible behaviors that combine tool scoping with prompt injection. Distinguish skills from plugins and from agent definitions — they overlap in surface but differ in role.

page_notes.title: Plugin System & Marketplace
page_notes.notes: Position plugins as first-class extensions that contribute to multiple registries (skills, hooks, agents, MCP, session-tools) through one manifest. Note that built-in plugins use the same format — there are no special cases.

page_notes.title: Agent Definition Registry
page_notes.notes: Discuss agents-as-data: markdown with frontmatter, capability-gated visibility, and how spawn_agent's agent_type lookup makes registered agents authoritative over LLM-supplied parameters.

page_notes.title: Widget Builder Builtin Plugin
page_notes.notes: Treat this as a worked example showing how capabilities, SessionTool, builtin plugin scanning, and the agent registry compose to deliver a real UX feature. Reader should leave understanding the integration shape, not the widget code.

page_notes.title: SessionTool Protocol
page_notes.notes: Explain why session-scoped tools differ from regular tools — per-agent state and the ability to signal loop exit. Plugin-contributable, but built-ins (like exit-plan-mode) use the same protocol to avoid special cases.

page_notes.title: Permissions, Hooks & Tool Execution Gates
page_notes.notes: Discuss how user trust and external integrations layer onto every tool call without the model needing to know. Emphasize error isolation: the runtime never lets a third-party hook block execution.

page_notes.title: Capabilities System & Feature Gating
page_notes.notes: Discuss who decides what the client can render and how that decision propagates without breaking older clients. The header is the contract; the filter is enforcement; the design is intentionally additive so missing capabilities fail silently.

page_notes.title: Slash Commands & User Commands
page_notes.notes: Position slash commands as a shared user-steering vocabulary that every interface inherits for free. Discuss the registry pattern, how commands return structured payloads that each surface renders natively, and why this keeps the CLI, console, and chat adapters consistent without duplicated logic.

page_notes.title: Tool Layer (mewbo_tools)
page_notes.notes: Frame the three tool families (local, MCP, LSP) and what unifies them under AbstractTool. Discuss result kind-discrimination as the rendering contract that lets new tool types render without frontend changes.

page_notes.title: Tool Registry & Spec System
page_notes.notes: Position ToolSpec as a contract object — schema, factory, and capabilities bundled together. Discuss filter_specs as the universal scoping mechanism reused by orchestrator, sub-agents, and skills.

page_notes.title: Local Tools (File Edit, Shell, Read)
page_notes.notes: Emphasize the safety guarantees (safe path resolution, approval gating for destructive operations) and the structured result contract that drives frontend rendering.

page_notes.title: Configurable Edit Tool Mechanism
page_notes.notes: Discuss why two edit formats exist (model preference varies by family) and how auto-detection removes the configuration burden for most users. Explicit config remains the override path.

page_notes.title: MCP Tool Discovery & Schema Validation
page_notes.notes: Discuss why connection pooling matters for stability, the rationale for project-scoped vs global config layering, and how schema coercion handles loose MCP server output at the boundary.

page_notes.title: Tool Search & Deferred Schemas
page_notes.notes: Lead with the context-window economics: every MCP schema bound upfront costs tokens on every turn, and large catalogs (dozens of MCP servers, hundreds of tools) make this unsustainable. Discuss the deferred-then-rebind design as a direct response to that pressure, and note the format mirrors Claude Code's pattern so the model recognises it from training.

page_notes.title: LSP & Code Intelligence
page_notes.notes: Center on passive diagnostics as the headline feature — errors surface without an extra LLM turn. Discuss graceful absence (LSP_AVAILABLE) so readers know the integration is non-required.

page_notes.title: Client Interfaces
page_notes.notes: Frame this section as 'every client is a different way to reach the same engine'. Discuss what is genuinely client-specific (rendering, approval style, polling vs in-process) and what is shared (event types, slash commands, capabilities). The reader should leave knowing where to look when adding a new interface vs modifying an existing one.

page_notes.title: REST API (mewbo_api)
page_notes.notes: Position the API as the bridge layer for browsers, voice, and chat platforms. Discuss auth, CORS, and how state lives in stores rather than the API itself, which is intentionally thin.

page_notes.title: API Endpoints, Polling & Steering
page_notes.notes: Discuss the polling-based async model and why it was chosen over SSE-required architectures. Cover the breadth of management surfaces (steering, fork, plugins, IDE) so reader sees the full operational picture.

page_notes.title: Chat Platform Adapters
page_notes.notes: Frame this as the extensibility surface for chat integrations: today Nextcloud Talk and Email, but the same shape covers Slack, Telegram, and any future platform. Discuss what the protocol requires of an adapter, what the shared pipeline provides for free (dedup, mention gating, session routing, slash commands), and the conventions a new platform implementer must respect.

page_notes.title: Web IDE Integration
page_notes.notes: Discuss why per-session ephemeral containers, why an nginx proxy is needed instead of direct port exposure, and how the deadline-based watchdog keeps cleanup self-contained.

page_notes.title: Notifications & Share Tokens
page_notes.notes: Discuss these as the two ways a session escapes its own bubble: notifications push outward to a user (alerts, completion signals), shares pull inward by exposing read-only views to outsiders. Cover the security model — what a share token grants and what it does not — and where each store fits in the API surface.

page_notes.title: Web Console (mewbo_console)
page_notes.notes: Treat the console as one of several interfaces, not the canonical one. Discuss why polling-based UX is simple and acceptable, and what the standout features (inline widgets, fork, IDE launch) add over a plain chat UI.

page_notes.title: Console UI Components
page_notes.notes: Discuss the kind-discriminated rendering pipeline: how new event types get new components without touching the dispatcher. Cover the card hierarchy as a design pattern, not a file inventory.

page_notes.title: Terminal CLI (mewbo_cli)
page_notes.notes: Emphasize the in-process runtime — no API hop — and what that simplifies. Discuss approval style choices and why Rich/Textual were chosen for terminal rendering.

page_notes.title: Home Assistant Integration (mewbo_ha_conversation)
page_notes.notes: Frame as the simplest possible interface — voice in, voice out, over HTTP. Useful as a worked example of what a minimal client must do.

page_notes.title: Configuration & Runtime Settings
page_notes.notes: Discuss the config discovery chain and why MEWBO_HOME exists. Cover Pydantic strictness (extra='forbid') and the conditional component enabling pattern that lets optional features stay truly optional.

page_notes.title: CI/CD, Docker & Docs Pipeline
page_notes.notes: Discuss multi-arch image builds, the post-init script extension mechanism, and the docs deploy pipeline. Reader should understand how to plug into each stage without reading the YAML.

page_notes.title: Testing Patterns & Quality Gates
page_notes.notes: Articulate the integration-first philosophy — real code paths preferred over mocks, with mocks reserved for I/O boundaries. Discuss the AsyncMock LLM boundary as the canonical pattern.

page_notes.title: Tracing, Logging & Diagnostics
page_notes.notes: Center on the 4-layer investigation methodology and when to use each layer. The reader should leave with a mental triage flowchart for debugging a production session.

page_notes.title: Building a New Client
page_notes.notes: Focus on minimum viable wiring: what's required, what's optional, and how to consume events incrementally. Useful as a checklist for someone porting the engine to a new surface.

page_notes.title: Glossary
page_notes.notes: Cover terms that appear across multiple pages without in-context definition, and Mewbo-specific overloads of common words. Keep entries short — one sentence each — so the page stays a quick lookup, not a second explanation.
